lecture_context.lecture_title: What We See and What We Value: AI with the Human Perspective

unit.name: Object recognition: from human vision to ImageNet and deep learning
unit.description: Reviews why object recognition became the canonical visual intelligence problem and traces the pre-deep-learning and deep-learning eras that made it practical.
unit.summary: Fei-Fei Li begins from human perception experiments, noting that people can detect targets in rapidly flashed scenes and categorize complex stimuli within roughly 150 ms, with specialized brain areas supporting object understanding [ts=297s] [ts=373s]. She uses this to motivate object recognition as a foundational computer vision task, then recounts the pre-deep-learning history: part-based compositional models were elegant but ineffective, while statistical machine learning introduced learnable parameters, data dependence, and models such as Bayes nets, random fields, and SVMs [ts=423s] [ts=564s]. The decisive unlock came from taking Biederman's estimate of tens of thousands of human-recognizable categories seriously, leading to ImageNet's scale and, later, the 2012 convergence of data, CNNs, and GPUs that made object recognition industrially mature [ts=692s] [ts=856s].

key_points.text: Humans can detect targets in unfamiliar scenes shown at 10 Hz, highlighting strong object-focused visual understanding.
Object recognition became a fundamental building block for visual intelligence in computer vision.
Part-based object recognition models from the 1970s-1990s were mathematically appealing but did not work well in practice.
Statistical machine learning reframed recognition as learning parameters from data rather than hand-tuning rules.
Biederman's conjecture that children know roughly 30,000 to 100,000 visual categories motivated scaling up datasets.
The 2012 convergence of ImageNet, CNNs, and GPUs is presented as the birth of deep learning for vision.

kp_catalog.name: Visual intelligence and object recognition
kp_catalog.description: Object recognition is treated as a core capability of both human and machine vision and as the starting point of modern computer vision progress. The lecture frames vision as central to animal and human intelligence, not merely one application area inside AI.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because visual intelligence and object recognition formalizes how object recognition is treated as a core capability of both human and machine vision and as the starting point of modern computer vision progress, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Data-driven supervised learning pipeline
kp_catalog.description: Pre-deep-learning progress came from shifting from hand-coded rules to learnable statistical models trained from data. The collect-train-predict paradigm: gather labeled data, fit a model, and evaluate predictions on unseen examples.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because data-driven supervised learning pipeline formalizes how pre-deep-learning progress came from shifting from hand-coded rules to learnable statistical models trained from data, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Deep learning resurgence from data, compute, and scale
kp_catalog.description: Why modern neural NLP depends on a set of training and modeling ideas that made deep networks workable after years of difficulty. Large-scale datasets, GPU computation, and neural architectures converged to unlock modern deep learning for vision.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because deep learning resurgence from data, compute, and scale formalizes how why modern neural NLP depends on a set of training and modeling ideas that made deep networks workable after years of difficulty, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
